steps: Start with $v = -\dfrac{w}{150x}$. Multiply both sides by $150x$: $150vx = -w$.

Since $v$, $w$, and $x$ are all distinct positive numbers, $-150vx$ is negative — this is consistent because $w$ positive and the right side negative would be a contradiction, confirming $w$ must be negative under the equation (or the problem acknowledges the algebraic relationship holds regardless of sign constraints on the product).

choices.explanation:
• Multiplying $v = -\frac{w}{150x}$ through by $150x$ gives $150vx = -w$, so $w = -150vx$.
  • This is a direct two-step rearrangement with no errors.
  • That matches choice A — A is correct.

• This would follow from $v = -\frac{w}{150x}$ only if we divided by $x^2$ instead of multiplying by $x$.
  • Multiplying correctly yields $w = -150vx$, not $-\frac{150v}{x}$.
  • Eliminated because $x$ should be in the numerator, not the denominator.

• Test by substituting back: $v = -\frac{-x/(150v)}{150x} = \frac{x}{150v \cdot 150x} = \frac{1}{150v \cdot 150}$, which does not simplify to $v$.
  • This form inverts the relationship between $v$ and $x$.
  • Eliminated because substituting back does not recover the original equation.

• This implies $v = -(v + 150x)/(150x)$, which expands to $-v/(150x) - 1$. That is not equivalent to the original equation.
  • Addition has no basis here; the original equation involves only multiplication and division.
  • Eliminated because no valid algebraic step converts a ratio into a sum.
